DiscoveryInterview is missing an interview date.

pages.text:
ZACH — Discovery Interview

Your Daily Work

What does your day look like from start to finish?

Answer:

I start each day by checking Microsoft Teams messages from Robert and Madison, then Outlook for emails from clients or other employees.

After that I move into active project work. My workload varies depending on priorities, but typically includes:
● Website development and maintenance
● Product spec sheets
● Product sign-off drawings
● Brochures
● Price sheets
● Working with Sagar on SphereController updates
● Other marketing or product-related projects as needed

What are you working on most of the time?

Answer:

My priorities change frequently based on business needs.

Currently, the majority of my time is spent designing, building, and launching new websites.

Other major ongoing projects include SphereController development and product documentation.

Walk me through what happens when a new project lands on your desk.

Answer:

Most new projects begin as a verbal conversation with Robert, although they may also come through Microsoft Teams or occasionally from Madison.

Once I understand the project requirements, I create the requested deliverable, which could include:
● Product spec sheets
● Product sign-off drawings
● Websites
● SphereController interface updates
● Brochures
● Price sheets
● Other design or marketing materials

What types of things do you make?

Answer:

I create a wide variety of materials for SSI, including:
● Product specification sheets
● Product sign-off sheets
● Technical drawings
● Websites
● SphereController interface graphics and updates
● Brochures
● Price sheets
● Excel pricing spreadsheets
● Flyers
● Posters
● Instagram graphics
● Shipping labels
● QR code labels
● Product display graphics
● Product mockups
● Laser-cut DXF designs
● Business Letters
● Intake forms

Inputs — What Comes To You

Who gives you work?

Answer:

Most work comes directly from Robert or Madison.

Occasionally customers request SphereController updates, but customer requests are relatively rare.

How do you receive briefs or instructions?

Answer:

Most instructions are delivered through:
● Microsoft Teams
● Verbal conversations

Occasionally projects may begin from emails or discussions.

How clear are the briefs you receive?

Answer:

The project requirements are usually clear.

For marketing materials such as websites, brochures, catalogs, and spec sheets, I frequently use ChatGPT to help write or expand body copy.

Occasionally I need additional information, such as:
● Product names
● Technical specifications
● Product measurements

How much back-and-forth happens before you start vs. after you deliver?

Answer:

Typically there is very little back-and-forth.

If the project is straightforward, I complete it, send it to Robert or Madison, and it is approved without additional revisions.

Revisions usually only happen if project requirements change.

Your Outputs — What You Deliver

List everything you create for SSI.

Answer:

Deliverables include:
● Product specification sheets
● Product sign-off sheets
● Technical drawings
● Websites
● SphereController updates
● Brochures
● Price sheets
● Excel pricing spreadsheets
● Flyers
● Posters
● Instagram graphics
● Shipping labels
● QR code labels
● Product display graphics
● Product mockups
● Laser-cut DXF files
● Formal Letters
● Intake forms

Who receives your work?

Answer:

Almost all completed work is delivered to Robert and Madison for review and approval.

How do you know when something is approved?

Answer:

Approval is usually communicated directly by Robert.

In many cases I deliver the work to Robert and Madison, and if no additional feedback is given, I consider the project complete.

What formats do you work in?

Answer:

Most projects are created using Adobe software.

Common deliverable formats include:
● PDF (most documentation)
● PNG
● JPG
● SVG (logos and vector graphics)
● PDF (vector artwork)
● DXF (laser cutting files)

Volume & Workload

On a typical week, how many different projects are you juggling?

Answer:

Typically three to four projects simultaneously.

Usually this includes:
● One large website project
● SphereController updates
● Several smaller projects such as spec sheets or sign-off drawings that are completed the same day.

What's your biggest time consumer?

Answer:

Website development is by far the largest time commitment, especially websites with many pages.

Are there things you're asked to do repeatedly that could be templated?

Answer:

Most repetitive work has already been templated.

Examples include:
● Product specification sheets
● Product sign-off sheets

Is there anything you're the only person who knows how to do that worries you?

Answer:

Website design and maintenance could become a bottleneck if many websites need to be launched at once.

Converting artwork into production-ready DXF files is also specialized work that can be difficult and time-consuming.

Quick knowledge of file and document locations on this computer.

Pain Points

What's the most frustrating part of your workflow?

Answer:

Converting millimeter measurements into inches for product sign-off drawings.

What do you get asked for most that takes the longest to produce?

Answer:

Large website projects and extensive pricing spreadsheets.

What information do you wish you had earlier in the process?

Answer:

Most of the time I already have everything I need to begin.

What tools or resources would make your work faster or better?

Answer:

At the moment I feel like I have the tools and resources I need.

Software You Use Every Day

List every tool, app, or software you use regularly.

Answer:

● Adobe Illustrator
● Adobe InDesign
● Adobe Photoshop
● Adobe Premiere Pro
● Adobe Acrobat
● Figma
● Microsoft Teams
● Slack
● Microsoft Outlook
● Microsoft Excel
● Microsoft Word
● WordPress
● Elementor

What are you most skilled in?

Answer:

My strongest skills are:
● Graphic Design
● Layout Design
● Website Design

Is there anything you want to learn or use that you haven't been able to?

Answer:

No specific answer provided at this time.

The Big Question

Is there anything about your role that SSI depends on that would break if you weren't here?

Answer:

Areas where I provide specialized knowledge include:
● Knowing where company files and design assets are stored
● Quickly creating product specification sheets
● Producing product sign-off drawings
● Website maintenance
● SphereController graphics and interface updates

What would you build or create for SSI if you had unlimited time?

Answer:

I would completely redesign and modernize the main SSI website, improving both its appearance and overall organization.

● Website development and maintenance

● Product sign-off drawings
● Brochures
● Price sheets
● Working with Sagar on SphereController updates
● Other marketing or product-related projects as needed

questions.question: Is there anything you're the only person who knows how to do that worries you?
questions.answer:

Website design and maintenance could become a bottleneck if many websites need to be launched at once.

Quick knowledge of file and document locations on this computer.
